title: Zero-Downtime PostgreSQL Migrations in Production
description: How to change a live PostgreSQL schema without taking the app down: the expand-and-contract pattern, online DDL with CONCURRENTLY and NOT VALID, batched backfills, and the lock monitoring that lets you do it with confidence.

items.title: Plan every change as expand, migrate, then contract
items.description: The whole game is never changing schema and code in one lockstep step. Expand: add the new thing without touching the old one. Migrate: backfill data and move reads and writes over while both shapes exist. Contract: drop the old thing once nothing reads it. A single migration that renames a column and ships matching code will break every old pod still running during the rollout. Split it into steps that each work with the code on both sides of the deploy.

codeBlocks.label: The three phases for adding email_verified

items.title: Set lock_timeout on every migration so a stuck DDL fails fast
items.description: This is the setting that saves you at 2am. Most DDL needs an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock. If a long-running query or an idle-in-transaction session holds the table, your ALTER waits for the lock, and every new query waits behind your ALTER. The whole table stalls. A short lock_timeout makes the migration give up and retry instead of taking the site down while it queues.

codeBlocks.label: Bound the lock wait and the statement runtime
Retry the migration instead of waiting forever
codeBlocks.code:
# Retry a few times so a brief lock contention isn't fatal
for attempt in 1 2 3 4 5; do
  psql "$DATABASE_URL" -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 -f migration.sql && break
  echo "attempt $attempt hit a lock, backing off..."
  sleep $((attempt * 5))
done

items.title: Add columns without rewriting the whole table
items.description: On Postgres 11 and newer, adding a column with a constant default is a metadata-only change and returns instantly, even on a huge table. The trap is a volatile default like now() or a function call, which still rewrites every row under an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock. If you need a computed or per-row value, add the column nullable first and backfill it as a separate step.

codeBlocks.label: Constant default fast, volatile default slow

items.title: Build indexes with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY
items.description: A plain CREATE INDEX holds a lock that blocks writes for as long as the build runs, which on a big table is minutes. CONCURRENTLY builds without blocking reads or writes. The cost: it can't run inside a transaction, it takes longer, and if it fails it leaves an INVALID index you have to drop and rebuild. Always check the index ended up valid before you rely on it.

codeBlocks.label: Concurrent build plus a validity check

items.title: Add NOT NULL with a CHECK constraint, not in one shot
items.description: ALTER TABLE ... SET NOT NULL scans the entire table while holding ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, so on a large table it blocks everything for the length of the scan. Instead add a CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) as NOT VALID, which is instant, then VALIDATE it under a lighter SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock that lets reads and writes continue. On PG 12+, a validated matching CHECK lets SET NOT NULL skip its own scan.

codeBlocks.label: NOT VALID then VALIDATE then SET NOT NULL

items.title: Add foreign keys in two steps with NOT VALID
items.description: Adding a foreign key normally locks both tables and scans the child table to check every existing row. Split it: ADD CONSTRAINT ... NOT VALID enforces the key for new and changed rows right away with only a short lock, then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT checks the existing rows under a SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock that doesn't block writes.

codeBlocks.label: Enforce now, validate existing rows later

items.title: Change a column type by adding a new column, not ALTER TYPE
items.description: ALTER COLUMN ... TYPE rewrites the entire table and rebuilds every index on it, all under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE. For anything but a tiny table that is an outage. The zero-downtime path is expand-and-contract: add a new column of the target type, write both, backfill the old rows, switch reads, then drop the old column and rename the new one into its place.

codeBlocks.label: Widen integer id to bigint without a rewrite

items.title: Backfill data in small batches, never one big UPDATE
items.description: A single UPDATE over millions of rows holds a transaction open for minutes, bloats the table with dead tuples, writes a wall of WAL that backs up replicas, and can deadlock with live traffic. Loop over the table in chunks, commit each batch, and sleep a little between them so autovacuum and replicas keep up. Make the batch idempotent so you can stop and resume any time.

codeBlocks.label: Procedure that commits each batch on its own

items.title: Make the app write both old and new shapes during the switch
items.description: During a rename or type change there is a window where old pods and new pods both run against the same database. If only new code writes the new column, every row the old pods insert is missing it. Deploy a step that writes both first, let it roll out fully, then backfill, then move reads. Decouple the column from the feature with a flag so you can turn reads on without another deploy.

codeBlocks.label: Write both columns, flip reads with a flag
codeBlocks.code:
# Transition step: write the old and the new column on every save
def save_email(conn, user_id, email):
    conn.execute(
        "UPDATE users SET email = %s, email_norm = %s WHERE id = %s",
        (email, email.strip().lower(), user_id),
    )

# Keep reads on the old column until the backfill is done,
# then flip with a flag instead of another deploy:
col = "email_norm" if flags.enabled("use_email_norm") else "email"

items.title: Never rename a live column or table in place
items.description: ALTER TABLE ... RENAME COLUMN is instant on the database, but it breaks every running query that still uses the old name the moment it commits. Old application pods start throwing 'column does not exist' until the rollout finishes. Treat a rename as expand-and-contract: add the new name, write both, backfill, switch reads, then drop the old one. For a table, leave a view behind so old code keeps working.

codeBlocks.label: Add-and-retire instead of renaming

items.title: Drop columns and tables only after nothing reads them
items.description: Dropping a column takes a brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock and is otherwise cheap, but if any running code still selects it, that code fails the instant you commit. Ship the code that stops using the column, confirm in query stats and logs that nothing references it, wait out a deprecation window past your rollback horizon, then drop. Once it is gone, getting it back means a restore, so there is no reason to rush.

codeBlocks.label: Confirm the column is unused before dropping

items.title: Use a migration tool that can run statements outside a transaction
items.description: Most tools wrap each migration in a transaction so a failure rolls back cleanly. But CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE, and a few others cannot run inside a transaction block and will error out. Pick a tool that lets you mark a migration non-transactional per file, and keep those statements in their own migration so one failure doesn't strand a half-applied batch.

codeBlocks.label: golang-migrate: one concurrent statement, no transaction
Flyway: disable the per-migration transaction
codeBlocks.code:
# V42__add_orders_index.sql.conf, next to the migration
executeInTransaction=false

items.title: Rehearse the migration on a production-sized copy and time the locks
items.description: A migration that finishes in 50ms on your laptop's 1,000-row table can hold ACCESS EXCLUSIVE for 20 minutes on the real 200-million-row table. The only way to know is to run it against a restore of production-sized data and measure. Run it, watch which locks it takes and for how long, and only then schedule it against prod.

codeBlocks.label: Inspect the lock a migration takes

items.title: Watch locks and blocking queries while the migration runs
items.description: When a migration stalls you need to know in seconds whether it is waiting on a lock and who holds it, so you can cancel the blocker or abort the migration before the queue of waiting queries takes the app down. Have these queries open in a second window before you start, not after the pager goes off.

codeBlocks.label: Find the blocker and cancel it
codeBlocks.code:
-- Who is blocking whom right now
SELECT blocked.pid   AS blocked_pid,
       blocking.pid  AS blocking_pid,
       blocked.query AS blocked_query,
       blocking.query AS blocking_query
FROM pg_stat_activity blocked
JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking
  ON blocking.pid = ANY (pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid));

-- Cancel a blocker gently, or terminate it if it won't let go
SELECT pg_cancel_backend(12345);
SELECT pg_terminate_backend(12345);

items.title: Watch replication lag and dead tuples during big backfills
items.description: A large backfill writes a lot of WAL and creates a lot of dead tuples. The WAL can push read replicas seconds or minutes behind, so anything reading from them serves stale data. The dead tuples bloat the table until autovacuum catches up. Watch both during the backfill and slow the batch rate down if a replica starts falling behind.

codeBlocks.label: Replica lag and table bloat at a glance
codeBlocks.code:
-- Replica lag in bytes and time, run on the primary
SELECT client_addr, state,
       pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), replay_lsn) AS lag_bytes,
       replay_lag
FROM pg_stat_replication;

-- Dead vs live tuples and last autovacuum for the table being backfilled
SELECT relname, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup, last_autovacuum
FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE relname = 'events';
